name: Kraken (SS-370)
events: This page dealing with the wartime history of USS Kraken was created in December 2025.
[No deck logs of this submarine are currently available to us so therefore some details, particularly regarding trials and exercises, might be missing on this page.]
11 Sep 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) commenced a trial and exercise period on the Great Lakes. (1)
27 Sep 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) departed Manitowoc for Chicago. (1)
28 Sep 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived at Chicago from Manitowoc. At Chicago she was placed in a special dry dock and the passage to New Orleans began in the evening. (1)

3 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived at New Orleans from Chicago. At New Orleans she left the special dry dock. The next day she proceeded to the Naval Base at Algiers, Louisiana for the loading of supplies, torpedoes, ammunition, fuel and water. (1)
8 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) departed New Orleans, Louisiana for the Panama Canal Zone.
She was escorted until position 28°45'N, 89°43'W by USS SC-512 (Lt.(jg) C.H. Stevenson, USNR), CG 83459 (?) and CG 83468 (?).

In that position the two Coast Guard patrol vessels parted company and USS Kraken continued on towards position 21°01'N, 83°30'W escorted by USS SC-512. From this position onwards USS Kraken proceeded unescorted.
13 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived at Coco Solo, Panama Canal Zone from New Orleans, Louisiana.

16 Oct 1944 In the afternoon, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) joined exercises in the Gulf of Panama which are being carried out by USS Sea Robin (T/Cdr. P.C. Stimson, USN), USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

17 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)
18 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN) and USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). (2)

19 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

22 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

23 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

24 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

25 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

26 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)
27 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN) and USS Mallard (T/Lt. B.L. Maddox, USN). (2)

28 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

29 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

30 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

31 Oct 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises in the Gulf of Panama with USS Alden (T/Lt.Cdr. W. Herkness, 2nd, USN), USS SC-541 (?) and USS SC-687 (Lt.(jg) T.L. Hickey, USNR). (2)

4 Nov 1944 After a training period, USS Blueback (T/Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN) and USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), departed the Panama Canal Zone for Pearl Harbor. En-route the two submarines carried out various exercises with each other.
21 Nov 1944 USS Blueback (T/Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN) and USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Balboa, Panama Canal Zone.
28 Nov 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Baron (Lt.Cdr. W.M. Dickerson, USNR). (3)
29 Nov 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with USS Sicard (Lt. J.W. Botten, USN) and USS Baron (Lt.Cdr. W.M. Dickerson, USNR). (4)
30 Nov 1944 During 30 November / 1 December 1944, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor with, at least, USS Sicard (Lt. J.W. Botten, USN). (5)
2 Dec 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
3 Dec 1944 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor.
5 Dec 1944 During 5/6 December 1944, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Pearl Harbor. These included night exercises.
12 Dec 1944 Around 1300 VW/12, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) departed from Pearl Harbor for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the South China Sea.
Before proceeding to her patrol area she was to proceed to Saipan to top off with fuel, water and provisions and to effect voyage repairs if required. (6)

19 Dec 1944 At 1535 L/19, north-east of Wake Island, in position 20°23'N, 167°45'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a friendly submarine. USS Kraken dived before calls could be exchanged. Estimated course of the other submarine was 060° at 15 knots.
Perhaps this may have been USS Searaven (T/Lt.Cdr. R. Berthrong, USN) en-route to Midway from patrol (arrived at Midway on 21 December). She did not report anything though. (6)
23 Dec 1944 Around 1530 K/23, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived at Saipan from Pearl Harbor.
She was escorted in by USS PC-1126 (Lt. J.F. Carrere, USNR) which had joined around 1000 K/23. (6)
24 Dec 1944 Around 1330 K/24, USS Kete (T/Cdr. R.L. Rutter, USN) departed from Saipan to resume her first war patrol. She was to patrol in the East China Sea off the Ryukyu Islands.
USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) was in company until 2300 K/25. USS Kraken was to patrol in the South China Sea, which was also her first war patrol.
They were escorted out until 1900 K/24 by USS PC-1126 (Lt. J.F. Carrere, USNR). (7)
1 Jan 1945 At 2230 H/1, while to the south-east of Hainan Island, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) entered her assigned patrol area. (6)

3 Jan 1945 At 0550 H/3, off the south coast of Hainan Island, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) picked up SJ radar interference which turned out to be coming from USS Sea Robin (T/Cdr. P.C. Stimson, USN). Recognition was exchanged with the SJ radar and some information was also exchanged. (8)
4 Jan 1945 At 2015 H/4, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received a signal from CTF 71 ordering her to patrol off the coast of Indo China. Course was set to comply with these orders. (6)
5 Jan 1945 At 0030 H/5, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) entered her new patrol area.

At 0516 H/5, in position 15°12'N, 109°09'E, radar contact was obtained on bearing 236° at a range of 4600 yards. Sight contact was made shortly afterwards. The contact appeared to be a small craft. No sails were up and it was not believed to be a sailing vessel. It was thought to be some sort of patrol vessel and was avoided. (6)

7 Jan 1945 At 2236 H/7, in position 15°54'N, 108°43'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) obtained radar contact on bearing 230° at a range of 6200 yards. The contact tracked on course 310° at 5 knots. It was thought to be the same ship, thought to be a small patrol vessel that had been contacted in the morning of the 6th. Once again it was avoided. (6)
12 Jan 1945 At 2200 H/12, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to perform lifeguard duties during a carrier strike on Hainan on the 14th.
At 0200 H/13, course was set to comply with these orders. (6)
14 Jan 1945 At 2100 H/14, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) left her lifeguard station to resume offensive patrol in accordance with new orders that were received at 1750 H/14. (6)

15 Jan 1945 At 2050 H/15, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to resume lifeguard duties on the 16th. She was to be in the same position for these duties as had been ordered for the lifeguard duties for the 14th. Course was set to return to position ordered. Due to the sea conditions it was not possible to be in the ordered position on time. It was expected that USS Kraken would arrive about an hour late. (6)
16 Jan 1945 At 0839 H/16, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived in the ordered position for the lifeguard duties.
At 1005 H/16, a Hellcat (Grumman F6F) was seen to make a crash landing about a mile and a half ahead. Course was set to close this position and try to rescue the pilot.

At 1027 H/16, the rescue was completed. Ens. R.W. Bertschi, USNR, was picked up. He was from the USS Lexington (T/Capt. E.W. Litch, USN). He was not injured and not suffering from exposure or exhaustion. Position in which he was picked up was 18°17'N, 110°24'E.

At 1140 H/16, information was received that a fighter pilot was down bearing 245° - 45 miles from 'Sugar Loaf Point'. Went to four engine speed and set course for the reported position which was a run of 75 miles. It was hoped to reach the survivor before 1600 H/16.
At 1615 H/16, USS Kraken arrived in the reported position and began to search the area but the reported downed pilot was not found. (6)
17 Jan 1945 At 1858 H/17, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) set course to return to her patrol area anticipating on receiving orders to do so.
At 2034 H/17, orders were received, as expected, to return to the patrol area and resume offensive patrol. (6)
18 Jan 1945 At 2120 H/18, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to patrol off Camranh Bay. Course was set accordingly. (6)

19 Jan 1945 At 0812 H/19, in position 14°16'N, 110°03'E, two lookouts on board USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) reported seeing a periscope. Course was changed and speed increased to run around this contact. (6)
20 Jan 1945 At 0100 H/20, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) entered her new patrol area. (6)

21 Jan 1945 At 0004 H/21, in position 12°02'N, 109°23'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) obtained radar contact on bearing 344° at a range of 13000 yards. Tracking was commenced. There were two targets, both were small pips at this rane. The targets tracked at 13 knots on a base course of 210°.
At 0058 H/21, USS Kraken closed to attack.
At 0113 H/21, the targets made a large zig towards when the range was 6000 yards. It was thought USS Kraken had been discovered and she turned away.
At 0120 H/21, the targets zigged away. Kraken changed course to the southward to gain a position ahead again.

At 0200 H/21, USS Kraken turned in to attack. The turn in was started too soon and a a range of 4200 yards the setup for a torpedo attack was found to be unfavourable. It was considered a waste of torpedoes to fire and course was changed to try to get ahead of the enemy once more.

At 0230 H/21, the targets entered shallow water. The new approach was broken off. It is believed the targets were either frigates or submarine chaser type vessels. Both pips were the same size and speed checked well at 13 knots. The pips appeared to be smaller than the ones made by US destroyer escorts at the same ranges.

At 1134 H/21, while conducting submerged patrol in position 11°39'N, 109°18'E, a mast was sighted bearing 347° at a range of 4.5 miles. The mast was drawing to the left. Changed course to 257° to try to intercept.
At 1148 H/21, the target was identified as an enemy patrol craft, trawler type. He was pinging and apparently on patrol.
At 1210 H/21, the enemy changed course to the northward. The closest range had been about 3500 yards.
At 1245 H/21, the vessel was lost from sight to the northward.

At 2001 H/21, now running on the surface in position 11°26'N, 109°10'E, USS Kraken obtained radar contact on bearing 269° at a range of 13000 yards. Commenced tracking and went to full speed on four engines. There were two targets. Pips were about the same size as those contacted early in the day. The targets tracked on course 210° at 15 knots.

At 2020 H/21, the targets, which had changed course to 250°, were heading into shallow water which they entered a little over half an hour later. It was once again believed these targets were frigates or submarine chasers.
At 2100 H/21, USS Kraken altered course to the northward to return to the Camranh Bay area. (6)

22 Jan 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) attacked what is identified as an escorted medium sized tanker to the north-east of Camranh Bay. Three torpedoes were fired but no hits were obtained. The attack appeared to have been unnoticed and no counter attack followed.
0723 H/22 - In position 12°02'N, 109°24'E, sighted masts bearing 012° at a range of 7 miles. Commenced approach.

The contact turned out to be a convoy made up of one medium tanker escorted by four patrol vessels. The convoy was under constant helm steering courses between 150° and 260°. Speed checked fairly well at 12 knots. One escort was on the beach side of the tanker and the other three were to seaward. Kraken was on the shore side about 500 yards off the target's track and waiting for the target to come in.

When the range to the target was 2500 yards and to the nearest escort about 1000 yards this escort headed almost directly towards. As he would pass close aboard Cdr. Henry had to let this escort go by before being able to have another look at the target.

The escort indeed passed close aboard to starboard. Range to the target was now seen to be 1000 yards with a 20° starboard angle on the bow. It was not possible to attack with the bow tubes so Kraken swung round for a stern tube attack. This took a while and when ready Kraken was 400 yards on the target beam. It was noted that the target was towing a sweep wire astern.

0800 H/22, in position 12°02'N, 109°22'E, fired three stern tubes (8,9 and 10) from a range of about 1750 yards for a torpedo run of 2100 yards. No hits were obtained. The torpedoes were all heard to run normally. The escorts did not detect them and there were no end of run explosions.
0848 H/22 - The convoy passed out of sight to the southward without ever apparently suspecting the presence of the submarine. (6)
24 Jan 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) attacked what is thought to be a Japanese frigate (Kaibokan) to the south of Camranh Bay. No hits were obtained.

2053 H/24 - In position 11°50'N, 109°25'E, obtained radar contact bearing 304° at a range of 14000 yards. Commenced tracking which gave the following result. There was one target zigging between courses of 250° and 170°. Speed 12.5 knots. Commenced an end around.
2237 H/24 - Commenced attack.
2245 H/24 - Sighted the target and a few minutes later identified it as a Japanese frigate at a range of 5000 yards.

2256 H/24 - In position 11°31'N, 109°07'E, fired four bow torpedoes from a range of about 3300 yards. No hits were obtained. The torpedoes were seen to breach during their run but the run was straight and there were no prematures and no end of run explosions. The target did not sight the submarine and did not turn away until after the torpedo, which passed ahead, had gone by.
Kraken retired on the surface while the target milled around on various courses. There was frantic signalling between him and the beach. (6)
25 Jan 1945 At 2200 H/25, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived on station near Cape Varella for lifeguard duties.

At 2220 H/25, SJ radar interference was picked up and shortly afterwards recognition signals were exchanged with USS Rock (T/Cdr. J.J. Flachsenhar, USN) which was leaving the area to return to Fremantle. (6)

26 Jan 1945 At 1809 H/26, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) left her lifeguard station to patrol 30 miles to the east on the Singapore - Hong Kong traffic route until required for lifeguard duties for the next upcoming air strike.

At 2020 H/26, SJ radar interference was noted and shortly afterwards recognition signals were exchanged with USS Hake (T/Cdr. F.E. Hayler, USN) which had just arrived in the area coming from Fremantle. (9)
27 Jan 1945 At 1258 H/27, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) was back in her lifeguard station.
At 1900 H/27, USS Kraken left her lifeguard station to return to patrol on the Singapore - Hong Kong traffic route. (6)

28 Jan 1945 At 1903 H/28, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) noted SJ radar interference and shortly afterwards recognition signals were exchanged with USS Flounder (T/Cdr. J.E. Stevens, USN) which had just arrived in the area from Fremantle.
At 2045 H/28, orders were received from CTF 71 ordering USS Kraken to start working her way towards Fremantle.

At 2315 H/28, USS Kraken sent a signal to CTF 71 requisting a five day extention of the patrol in this area. It was decided to patrol in the current position until a reply was received. Permission was granted the following evening. A coordinated patrol with USS Pargo (T/Lt.Cdr. D.B. Bell, USN) was ordered. USS Pargo having just arrived in the area coming from Fremantle. T/Cdr. Henry being in command. Course was then set to proceed to the Camranh Bay area. (10)

30 Jan 1945 At 0817 H/30, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a submarine bearing 180° at a range of 7000 yards. It was identified as a US submarine and subsequently recognition signals were exchanged with USS Pargo (T/Lt.Cdr. D.B. Bell, USN). Information on the area and patrol orders were passed. USS Kraken would patrol inshore while USS Pargo would patrol offshore until the second when positions would be exchanged. (11)
1 Feb 1945 At 2300 H/1, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) left the inshore patrol to proceed to patrol offshore tomorrow. (6)
2 Feb 1945 At 2000 H/2, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) left patrol to proceed to Fremantle. (6)

4 Feb 1945 At 1027 H/4, while running on the surface east of the Riau Islands, in position 02°50'N, 106°55'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a periscope bearing 160° at a range of 2000 yards. Kraken went ahead flank and ran around the contact. (6)

5 Feb 1945 At 0910 H/5, while spending the day submerged to the north of the Karitmata Strait, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a submarine bearing 252° at a range of 5 miles. The submarine was identified as US and believed to have been USS Flasher (T/Cdr. G.W. Grider, USN) on her way to her patrol area coming from Fremantle.
This may well have been a correct identification as USS Flasher had completed the northbound passage of the Karimata Strait at 0525 H/5. (6)

5 Feb 1945 At 0017 H, in position 00°17'N, 106°27'E, USS Hardhead (T/Cdr. F.A. Greenup, USN) sighted and exchanged calls with the southbound USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN). [Nothing is mentioned on this in the patrol report of USS Kraken.]

At 1729 H/5, a signal was received which started that the air strikes scheduled for the next day were cancelled. USS Hardhard set course for Karimata Strait to proceed to Fremantle. Later it was decided to call at Onslow before proceeding to Fremantle due to fuel shortage. (12)

7 Feb 1945 At 1750 H/7, While proceeding submerged in position 06°11'S, 114°17'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), sighted smoke bearing 122° at a range of 10 miles. This contact was subsequently identified as a small auxiliary schooner with a low deck house aft. It was not a suitable target for a torpedo attack and was left alone. (6)

8 Feb 1945 At 1812 H/8, while proceeding submerged toward the northern entrance to Lombok Strait, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a submarine bearing 070° at a range of 5 miles. The contact was shortly afterwards identified as a US submarine.

At 1845 H/8, USS Kraken surfaced and exchanged recognition signals by SJ radar with this submarine which turned out to be USS Perch (T/Cdr. B.C. Hills, USN) which was also en-route to Fremantle. USS Perch gives the position of the encounter as 07°20'S, 116°33'E. (13)

8 Feb 1945 At 2100 H/8, while about to commenced the southbound transit of Lombok Strait on the surface, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received a signal that an aircraft carrier had been reported in the Makassar Strait. It was decided to break off the attempt to pass Lombok Strait and proceed to the north to patrol off Sekala Island until tomorrow evening in the hope of making contact. (6)
9 Feb 1945 At 0500 H/9, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) noted radar interference and shortly afterwards recognition signals were exchanged with USS Gabilan (T/Cdr. W.B. Parham, USN).
USS Gabilan reported this taking place at 0304 H/9. (14)
9 Feb 1945 At 2230 H/9, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) commenced the southbound transit of Lombok Strait on the surface.

At 2253 H/9, in position 08°22'S, 115°50'E, radar contact was obtained bearing 202° at a range of 5900 yards. The contact developed into two small pips which were identified as patrol craft. They tracked on course 260° at a speed of 5 knots for about 5 minutes and then on course 070°. USS Kraken ran around the contacts.
At 0030 H/10, USS Kraken cleared Lombok Strait. (6)

10 Feb 1945 At 0854 H/10, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a US submarine bearing 146° at a range of 12 miles. Recognition signals were exchanged with the northbound USS Blenny (T/Cdr. W.H. Hazzard, USN).
USS Blenny reported the encounter at the same time in position 10°48'S, 115°29'E. (15)
10 Feb 1945 At 1027 H/10, in position 11°12'S, 115°18'E, USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN) exchanged calls with the SJ radar with the southbound USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN). (16)
14 Feb 1945 Around 0915 H/14, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) ended her 1st war patrol at Fremantle, Australia.
She was escorted in by HMAS Inverell (Lt. A.I. Chapman, RANR(S)) which had joined around 0510 H/14.
1 Mar 1945 From 1 to 10 March 1945, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted a training period off Fremantle. (17)
3 Mar 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Fremantle with, at least, HMAS Kiama (T/Lt. S.J. Benson, RANR(S)). (18)

7 Mar 1945 USS Gurnard (T/Lt.Cdr. G.S. Simmons, 3rd, USN), USS Perch (T/Cdr. B.C. Hills, USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted exercises off Fremantle with, at least, HMAS Kiama (T/Lt. S.J. Benson, RANR(S)). (18)
15 Mar 1945 Around 1500 H/15, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) departed from Fremantle for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the South China Sea.
Before proceeding on patrol exercises were carried out with USS Corpus Christie (Cdr. W.W. Childress, USCG). These exercises were completed at 2200 H/15.
17 Mar 1945 At 1427 H/15, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) passed the southbound USS Blower (T/Cdr. J.H. Campbell, USN). (17)
17 Mar 1945 At 2050 H/15, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) exchanged recognition signals the southbound USS Pintado (T/Cdr. B.A. Clarey, USN). (17)
19 Mar 1945 At 1210 H/19, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received a message from USS Croaker (T/Cdr. W.B. Thomas, USN), which was to the north of Kraken, requesting a rendezvous for radar tests.

At 2000 H/19, recognition signals were exchanged between the two submarines and the radar tests commenced. Almost four hours later the tests were completed and the submarines parted company. USS Kraken continued her northward passage while USS Croaker continued her southward passage to Exmouth Gulf where repairs to her SJ radar set were to be undertaken. (19)
21 Mar 1945 At 2343 H/20, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) commenced the northbound transit of Lombok Strait.

At 0157 H/21, in position 08°26'S, 115°49'E, radar contact was obtained bearing 280° at a range of 8000 yards. It was believed the contact was a patrol craft and USS Kraken ran around it on the surface.
At 0240 H/21, USS Kraken cleared Lombok Strait. (17)

22 Mar 1945 At 1557 H/22, west-north-west of Makassar, in position 04°58'S, 118°58'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted masts bearing 195° at a range of 10 miles. Kraken commenced to close to investigate.
At 1628 H/22, the ship was identified as a properly marked hospital ship believed to be the Takasago Maru (9315 GRT, built 1937).
At 1629 H/22, the approach was broken off. Photographs were taken off this vessel through the periscope.

This was however not the Takasago Maru which was in Japanese home waters at this time. It was the Hikawa Maru No.2 (6076 GRT, built 1927, former Tenno Maru, former Dutch Op Ten Noort) which arrived at Makassar on this day. (20)
25 Mar 1945 At 2350 H/25, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to conduct lifeguard duties off Singapore on 28 March. (17)
26 Mar 1945 At 0940 H/26, north-west of the Karimata Strait, in position 01°28'S, 107°46'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a sail bearing 320° at a range of about 11 miles.
At 0950 H/26, it was seen the this vessel had a rather heavy looking foremast which was considered to be suspicious. It was decided to close submerged to investigate.
At 1050 H/26, after having closed sufficiently it was seen that it was just a regular sailing vessel after all. It was not attacked. (17)

28 Mar 1945 At 0115 H/28, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received information that the lifeguard duties off Singapore were postponed until 30 March. Also patrol orders were given for the next two days.
At 0300 H/28, information was received that the air strike now was scheduled for 0030 H/29.
At 1900 H/28, orders were received to be on lifeguard station for a second Singapore air strike at 0230 H/31.
At 2015 H/28, information was received that this second strike would take flace at 0200 H/30. (17)
31 Mar 1945 With the lifeguard duties now completed, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) entered her patrol area at 0700 H/31. (17)
1 Apr 1945 At 0530 H/1, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) exchanged recognition signals with USS Caiman (T/Lt.Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN). [USS Caiman reported this as taking place at 0555 H/1] (21)

4 Apr 1945 At 0835 H/4, south-east of the Gulf of Siam, in position 06°40'N, 105°16'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a periscope bearing 045° at a range of 1500 yards. This periscope was put astern and the range was opened at speed.
The only possible friendly contact should have been HMS Tradewind (Lt. J. Nash, DSC, RN). [The 0800 I/4, position of HMS Tradewind was 00°53'S, 107°58'E so it certainly was not her.] (17)

13 Apr 1945 At 2130 H/13, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to perform lifeguard duties off Singapore during air attacks on 14, 15, 16 and 17 April. Course was set to proceed to the assigned lifeguard station on which USS Kraken arrived around 1100 H/14. (17)
17 Apr 1945 At 1200 H/17, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) completed her lifeguard duties. Course was set to return to her patrol area. (17)

20 Apr 1945 At 2201 H/20, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to proceed to the northeastern part of her patrol area and be on standby for lifeguard duties on the following day. Course was set to comply with these orders and speed was increased to four engine speed. (17)
21 Apr 1945 At 1800 H/21, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to proceed to perform lifeguard duties for an air strike on Saigon on 22 April. (17)
22 Apr 1945 At 1000 H/22, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) arrived on her assigned station to perform lifeguard duties for an air strike on Saigon.
At 2100 H/22, orders were received for lifeguard duty on 23 April. (17)
23 Apr 1945 At 2000 H/23, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) left patrol to proceed to Subic Bay. (17)
26 Apr 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) ended her 2nd war patrol at Subic Bay, Philippines.
She was escorted in by USS Jaccard (Lt.Cdr. C.R. Hamilton, USNR) which had joined around 0545 H/26.
At Subic Bay, USS Kraken was taken in had for a refit by USS Anthedon (T/Capt. R.E. Hawes, USN). During the refit USS Kraken was also docked in a floating dock. (17)
5 May 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) is docked in ARD 12 at Subic Bay. (22)
7 May 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) is undocked. (22)
12 May 1945 From 12 to 17 May 1945, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted a training period off Subic Bay. (23)
19 May 1945 At 1400 I/19, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) departed from Subic Bay for her 3rd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Siam.
She was escorted out until 2145 I/19, by USS Doyle C. Barnes (Lt.Cdr. J.P. Ingle, Jr., USNR) with whom also training exercises were carried out.
23 May 1945 At 2105 I/23, SJ interference was noted by USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) and shortly afterwards recognition calls were exchanged with USS Brill (T/Cdr. H.B. Dodge, USN).
At 2135 I/23, the two submarines made rendezvous and a bag of mail was transferred by USS Kraken to USS Brill. (24)

24 May 1945 At 1108 H/24, while patrolling submerged USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a periscope bearing 340° at a range of 4 miles. This is thought to have possibly been either USS Cobia (T/Cdr. A.L. Becker, USN) or USS Bergall (T/Cdr. J.M. Hyde, USN). (25)
24 May 1945 Around 0420 I/24, in position 07°20'N, 103°00'E, USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. F.W. Scanland, Jr., USN) and USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) made rendezvous.
USS Kraken, fresh out of Subic Bay, transferred a bag of very welcome mail to USS Hawkbill. (26)

24 May 1945 At 2137 I/24, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) made rendezvous with USS Bergall (T/Cdr. J.M. Hyde, USN). T/Cdr. Hyde was the leader of the pack USS Kraken was to be part of. Information was passed regarding the area and planned operations. (25)

25 May 1945 At 0642 I/25, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) made rendezvous with USS Cobia (T/Cdr. A.L. Becker, USN). Mail and hydraulic oil was transferred by USS Kraken to USS Cobia before both submarines continued their patrols. USS Cobia's patrol report gives the position of the rendezvous as 08°11'N, 102°52'E. (27)
27 May 1945 At 1415 I/27, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted USS Bugara (T/Cdr. A.F. Schade, USN) bearing 175° at a range of 9 miles.
The two submarines made rendezvous at 1450 I/27 and mail was transferred by USS Kraken to USS Bugara. (28)
30 May 1945 At 0334 I/30, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted gunfire bearing 205°. Kraken commenced to close to investigate.
At 0430 I/30, contact was made with USS Bergall (T/Cdr. J.M. Hyde, USN) which stated that she had just sunk two tugs that had been towing barges.
Both submarines then continued their patrol in a scouting line. (23)

31 May 1945 At 0404 I/31, in position 06°05'N, 102°30'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted masts bearing 185° at a range of 6 miles. On closing this were seen to be around twenty-four small native fishing vessels which were not attacked. (23)

2 Jun 1945 At 1934 I/2, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. F.W. Scanland, Jr., USN) bearing 320° at a range 7 miles. The two submarines closed and mail was transferred by USS Kraken to USS Hawkbill which was due to leave patrol to proceed to Fremantle. (23)
9 Jun 1945 At 1341 I/9, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted USS Cobia (T/Cdr. A.L. Becker, USN) bearing 030° at a range of 8 miles.

At 1420 I/9, the submarines made rendezvous and USS Kraken transferred mail to USS Cobia. USS Cobia was to leave patrol to proceed to Fremantle. USS Cobia gives the position of the rendezvous as being in 01°30'N, 106°30'E. (27)
11 Jun 1945 At 1830 I/11, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a submarine bearing 200° at a range of 7 miles.
Ten minutes later recognition signals were exchanged with HMS Thorough (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR). [The 2000 I/11 position of HMS Thorough was 01°36'N, 106°38'E.] (29)
12 Jun 1945 At 1645 I/12, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a submarine bearing 245° at a range of 7 miles.

Later recognition signals were exchanged with HMS Thorough (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR). [The logbook of HMS Thorough gives this as taking place at 1910 I/11. The 2000 I/11 position of HMS Thorough was 01°35'N, 106°31'E.] (29)
12 Jun 1945 At 1900 I/12, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) bearing 025° at a range of 9 miles.
The two submarine closed and information was exchanged. (30)

15 Jun 1945 At 0620 I/15, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) and USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) made rendezvous. Patrol instructions were transferred from USS Bullhead to USS Kraken after which the submarines parted company to continue their patrols. (30)
16 Jun 1945 At 0050 I/16, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) noted SJ radar interference and shortly afterwards calls were exchanged with USS Caiman (T/Lt.Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN).
At 1846 I/16, just to the north of the Karimata Strait, USS Kraken sighted a US submarine bearing 135° at a range of 9 miles. After exchanging calls this again proved to be USS Caiman. (31)

17 Jun 1945 At 0034 I/17, in Karimata Strait USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) noted SJ radar interference and subsequently made radar contact on bearing 185° at a range of 6 miles. Calls were transmitted but no answer came. This must have been the northbound USS Chub (T/Cdr. C.D. Rhymes, Jr., USN). (23)

18 Jun 1945 At 0423 I/18, north of the Thousand Islands, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) noted SJ radar interference and shortly afterwards calls were exchanged with USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN).
At 2045 I/18, north of Pulau Babi, just to the east of the northern entrance to the Sunda Strait, USS Kraken closed USS Bullhead to receive new patrol orders.
At 2250 I/18, USS Kraken received a contact report from USS Bullhead. Course was set to close to intercept.
At 0027 I/19, on closing USS Bullhead it was learnt that her contact had returned to port and course was set to return to Kraken's patrol station. (23)
19 Jun 1945 At 0915 I/19, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted smoke over to horizon on bearing 140°. Kraken commenced to close to investigate.

At 1010 I/19, the smoke had not been seen for half an hour. If a target was on the move it would have been reported by USS Bullhead (T/Cdr. W.T. Griffith, USN) by now. USS Kraken broke off the approach and returned to her patrol station.
At 1300 I/19, three columns of smoke were seen over the horizon bearing 144°. These were drawing to the westward. USS Kraken commenced to close to investigate.

At 1411 I/19, in position 05°48'S, 106°06'E, two Japanese motor launch type vessels were seen close aboard. The closest one being only 1000 yards away. USS Kraken went deep and commenced evasive tactict.
At 1515 I/19, on return to periscope depth there was no more sign of the two Japanese vessels nor of the three columns of smoke.
At 1607 I/19, USS Bullhead was sighted bearing 230° at a range of 5 miles. She was seen to submerge six minutes later.

At 2035 I/19, USS Kraken made rendezvous with USS Bullhead and learnt from her that the smoke seen had been from three coastal steamers and two escorts. Bullhead had sunk two of the coasters and had driven the third into Merak Roads were two other small ships were already moored. All three of these vessels were attacked and damaged with gunfire until ammunition ran out. USS Kraken was ordered to close their position and finish the job started by USS Bullhead. Course was thus set to close Merak Roads. (23)

20 Jun 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sank the small Japanese coastal steamer Tachibana Maru No.58 with gunfire in Sunda Strait, off Merak, Netherlands East Indies in position 05°56'S, 106°00'E. A second coastal steamer was claimed to have, at least, been damaged. Also damage was claimed to the dock and the surrounding area.
0225 I/20 - In position 05°56'S, 106°00'E commenced firing with the 5" gun and 40mm guns at two vessels, dock and dock area.
0305 I/20 - Ceased firing and entered the Sunda Strait to patrol there.

Fourty-four rounds of 5" and fifty-two rounds of 40mm had been fired from an average range of 2100 yards. One coastal steamer was destroyed as it burst into flames from fore to end. Another coastal steamer was damaged if not sunk but this was difficult to observe. The dock and surrounding area were certainly also damaged by the gunfire. Two hours after the bombardment fires were still seen while USS Kraken was already thirty miles away.
22 Jun 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) bombarded the Anjer Point lighthouse to the south of Merak, Java, Netherlands East Indies but apparently for little result.

While attempting to pass through the narrows at the northern end of the Sunda Stait to re-enter the Java Sea, USS Kraken was detected and fired upon by a Japanese shore battery. T/Cdr. Henry turned round and decided to spent another day patrolling in the Sunda Strait and make another attempt during the next night (which eventually did not happen).

Between 0033 I/22 and 0044 I/22 - USS Kraken bombarded the Anjer Point lighthouse. Fire was ceased when it was determined that little result was obtained and that further shooting would be be a waste of ammunition.

Five 5" hits were seen in the target area and also many 40mm hits were obtained. Despite that the damage inflicted appeared to be small and the action was thus broken off after ten rounds of 5" and thirty-two rounds of 40mm had been fired.

0053 I/22 - An enemy shore battery on Thwartway Island (in the northern entrance to the Sunda Strait) opened fire on USS Kraken. Course was changed to the southward to open the range. About fifteen rounds were fired but none was very close. There was only one gun firing and it was off rather small caliber. This gun was located on the Java side of Thwartway Island and the south-eastern end of the island. T/Cdr. Henry decided to patrol in the Sunda Strait for one more day and return to the Java Sea during the next night.

1117 I/22 - While west of Pulau Legundi, in position 05°50'S, 105°12'E, smoke was seen on bearing 349° at a range of about 3 miles. This turned out to be a small steam launch entering Legundi Strait from the west. It was not a suitable torpedo or gun target.

1430 I/22 - Now to the south-west of Pulau Legundi, in position 05°52'S, 105°10'E, smoke was sighted over the horizon bearing 090° at a range of 7 miles. This contact developed into a convoy of eight ships consisting of one small tanker, two coastal steamers, one tug with one barge in tow and three patrol type escort vessels.

Commenced approach. The convoy was on a base course of 265° at a speed of 5 knots and USS Kraken was on track. When the range had closed to 3000 yards Kraken pulled off track for a stern tube shot. The convoy was allowed to go by at a range of 1200 to 1500 yards. The targets were seen to be small and torpedoes may run too deep so believed that a night surface attack would offered the best possibility of success. It was easy to maintain contact submerged until dark and there was no harbour or protected anchorage into wich the convoy could escape along it's immediate route. If the night surface attack would fail there was then still a possibility to make a gun attack. So USS Kraken continued to trail the convoy during the remainder of the afternoon.
2028 I/22 - Surfaced. The convoy was now bearing 280° at a range of 10 miles. commenced an end around. (23)

23 Jun 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) claims to have sunk two small Japanese vessels, a tanker and steamer, out of a Japanese coastal convoy just to the west of the off the southern entrance to the Sunda Strait.

0000 I/23 - USS Kraken was now in a position ahead of the convoy. The night was bright and clear with no clouds and almost full moon. In order to get into a favourable firing position undetected it was decided to wait until the convoy rounded Vlakke Hoek and started up the southern coast of Sumatra. Little Furtune Island [now called Pulau Betuah] could be used as cover and background when Kraken would make her approach. Downside was that the attack had to be made in 8 to 12 fathoms of water but it was the only chance of an undetected approach.

0030 I/23 - Went to battle stations and commenced closing the convoy which was in loose column with one escort ahead, one on the flank closest to USS Kraken and one on the quarter on the side of USS Kraken.
0050 I/23 - In position 05°51'S, 104°28'E, commenced firing three bow torpedoes from 2850 yards. These torpedoes were fired at the largest ship.
0051 I/23 - Fired two bow torpedoes at the next ship in line from 2250 yards.

0051 I/23 - The flank escort, the nearest escort, turned towards and inceased speed when the fourth torpedo was fired. Immediately after firing torpedoes USS Kraken commenced to swing round to bring the stern tubes to bear.
0052 I/23 - The leading escort had now also turned towards and was also increasing speed.

0052 I/23 - Two torpedo hits were seen. One on the first target which was seen to disintegrate, and one on the second target. A third explosion was heard at this time but this was thought to have been a depth charge.
0053 I/23 - Commenced firing the four stern torpedoes from a range of 2300 yards at the now closing escorts. No hits were obtained.
Fire was also opened with the 40mm gun. Both escorts closing also opened fire. A small fire was started on the closest escort, obviously it had been hit.

0054 I/23 - USS Kraken steadied on her retirement course while going ahead at flank speed on all four engines. The escort on fire had sheered away but the other escort had closed to 1500 yards and was still firing. He was now raked with 20mm fire but still continued on his course still firing.

0055 I/23 - Range was opening now. The nearest escort was still shooting but his gunfire was getting wild while he was still being raked with 20mm fire. The second escort was seen smoking and was dropping aft rapidly.

0057 I/23 - Range was opening steadily now although both escorts were still firing and Kraken was still firing on them. A radar check showed only six targets on the screen instead of the eight before the attack so T/Cdr. Henry felt sure that the second target that had been hit had also been sunk.
25 Jun 1945 At 1955 I/25, USS Kraken left patrol to return to Fremantle. (23)

30 Jun 1945 At 1028 I/30, USS Kraken arrived at Onslow where she embarked 12702 gallons of diesel oil and 400 gallons off fresh water before leaving at 1405 I/30 to continue her passage to Fremantle. (23)
3 Jul 1945 USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) ended her 3rd war patrol at Fremantle.
She had been joined by an escort at 0600 I/3, which escorted her into port. [Unable to identify this escort vessel.]
19 Jul 1945 From 19 to 26 July 1945, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) conducted a training period off Fremantle. (32)

29 Jul 1945 Around 1430 H/29, USS Icefish (T/Cdr. R.W. Peterson, USN) and USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) departed from Fremantle for their 5th and 4th war patrols respectively. They were ordered to patrol in the South China Sea and Java Sea respectively.

On departure exercises were carried out with USS Corpus Christie (Cdr. W.W. Childress, USCG) and USS SC-739 (Lt.(jg) H. Richardson, Jr., USNR) until 2200 H/29. The submarines then set course for Lombok Strait carrying out more exercises en-route. (33)

31 Jul 1945 At 0715 I/31, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received orders to conduct lifeguard duty on 2 August. Speed was increased to confirm with these orders and to arrive on the assigned station on time.
At 1645 I/31, orders were received which cancelled the lifeguard duties. Speed was reduced to two engine speed. (32)

1 Aug 1945 At 0045 I/1, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) noted SJ radar interference and shortly afterwards recognition calls were exchanged with the southbound USS Cabrilla (T/Lt.Cdr. H.C. Lauerman, USN). (32)
4 Aug 1945 At 0025 I/4, in position 08°50'S, 115°42'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) obtained radar contact on bearing 080° at a range of 9200 yards. USS Kraken commenced tracking this contact.

The contact tracked on course 185° at 15 knots. One US and two British submarines were known to be in the area. Given the speed of the contact it could not have been either of the British submarines so it was considered the contact was most likely USS Brill (T/Cdr. H.B. Dodge, USN). No SJ radar interference was however noted.
At 0050 I/4, the contact was tracking on course 175° at 11 knots. USS Kraken meanwhile had commenced an end around. Still no SJ radar interference was picked up.
At 0130 I/4, a signal was sent to USS Brill on wolfpack frequence giving information on our contact and asking if it was her.
At 0150 I/4, confirmation came that this was indeed USS Brill and that her SJ radar was defective.

At 0200 I/4, USS Kraken resumed the northbound passage of Lombok Strait. It was no longer possible to complete the passage submerged during this night so it was decided to patrol submerged to the south of Lombok Strait and make another attempt to make the northbound passage on the surface the following night.
At 2203 I/4, USS Kraken commenced the northbound passage of Lombok Strait on the surface.
At 0008 I/5, the passage had been completed. Nothing had been seen of the enemy. (32)
5 Aug 1945 At 1900 I/5, near Kangean Island, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) heard two British submarines communicating underwater.

No exact position of USS Kraken is given in her patrol report but the two British submarines were most likely HMS Taciturn (Lt.Cdr. E.T. Stanley, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Thorough (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) which were operating in this area.

At 1945 I/5, a British submarine was seen to surface. This could have been either one of the above which surfaced at 2019 I/5 in position 07°01'S, 115°12'E and 2002 I/5 in position 07°02'S, 115°23'E respectively. (34)

6 Aug 1945 At 1330 I/6, proceeding submerged to the south of Kangean Island, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) sighted a British submarine bearing 079° at a range of 3500 yards. The submarine was proceeding westwards.
At 1455 I/6, a British submarine was sighted bearing 049° at a range of 5000 yards. This submarine was also proceeding westwards.
HMS Taciturn (Lt.Cdr. E.T. Stanley, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Thorough (Lt. A.G. Chandler, RNR) were proceeding westwards through this area at this time.
The first submarine sighted was most likely HMS Taciturn, the second one was most likely HMS Thorough. (34)
6 Aug 1945 At 2330 I/6, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) entered her patrol area. (32)

7 Aug 1945 At 1315 I/7, off the north coast of central Java, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) heard pinging bearing 195°. This pinging was very faint but seems to be drawing to the westward. Nothing was seen. Set course towards Cape Indramayu to try to intercept the enemy there in the evening.
At 2300 I/7, USS Kraken arrived off Cape Indramayu and commenced to patrol in the area but nothing developed. (32)

10 Aug 1945 At 2335 I/10, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) picked up SJ radar interference and shortly afterwards calls were exchanged with HMS Tiptoe (A/Lt.Cdr. R.L. Jay, RN) and HMS Trump (A/Lt.Cdr. A.A. Catlow). Both these submarines had been patrolling in the Sunda Strait area and had just been ordered to leave patrol, proceed eastwards and work their way towards Fremantle.
T/Cdr. Henry decided to shift patrol to the area between the Thousand Islands and the coast of Sumatra. (32)

11 Aug 1945 At 0120 I/11, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) made rendezvous with USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN). It was arranged that USS Lamprey would patrol the area to the east of the Thousand Islands and that USS Kraken would patrol to the west of the Thousand Islands and the coast of Sumatra until the 15th when the two submarines would change stations. (35)

13 Aug 1945 At 0237 I/13, in position 04°30'S, 106°15'E, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) obtained radar contact bearing 350° at a range of 22000 yards. Commenced tracking. There were two targets, both of the same size. They were tracked for about an hour and it was determined that the course of the targets was 170° at a speed of 9 knots. From the size of the pips the targets were thought to be Japanese frigates (Kaibokan) or larger. USS Kraken was in very shallow water and had 60 miles to run to reach deeper water. It was decided not to attack now and pull ahead on the projected track of the enemy for a daylight submerged attack.
At 0330 I/13, USS Kraken ceased tracking and commenced pulling ahead.
At 0727 I/13, USS Kraken dived south-east of Jason Rock.

At 0843 I/13, in position 05°30'S, 106°18'E, smoke was sighted bearing 137°. No effort was made to close or approach because the heavy black smoke looks suspiciously like an escort coming out to join the inbound ships. The bearing of the smoke was drawing to the left putting this vessel on a northerly course. It was decided to wait until the first contact of the day would show up on the scene.
At 1037 I/13, while at periscope depth in position 05°37'S, 106°18'E, pinging from two ships was heard on bearing 006°. Nothing was sighted on that bearing.
At 1045 I/13, the other escort, that had been first detected at 0843 I/13 had also commenced pinging and was closing the position of USS Kraken. Patrolling aircraft had also been sighted all morning.

At 1100 I/13, It became obvious that USS Kraken had been detected. The single escort was still searching while coming towards. His screws could now clearly be picked up. USS Kraken went to 90 feet and rigged for depth charges.

At 1110 I/13, all three escorts were now pinging at short scale and obviously searching in the area. The screws of all three could be clearly heard. USS Kraken commenced evasive tactics opening up to the south-west as much as possible. The hunt continued for about two hours and several times it was thought that the enemy was starting a depth charge run but these were never carried through.
By 1325 I/13, the pinging was now at a greater distance and long scale. Kraken started to come up to periscope depth.

At 1328 I/13, while at 70 feet, a series of explosion occurred. USS Kraken went back to 90 feet. These were thought to have been either aircraft bombs a long way off or small charges dropped close to Kraken.
By 1400 I/13, there was no evidence that the three escort vessels were closing again so USS Kraken had returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
At 1935 I/13, USS Kraken surfaced. (32)
14 Aug 1945 At 0126 I/14, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) received a signal that a British Liberator bomber was down in the eastern part of the Sunda Strait.

At 0237 I/14, USS Kraken had completed the southbound transfer of the northern entrance to the Sunda Strait and commenced a search for the crew of the downed aircraft. They were however not found. (32)

15 Aug 1945 At 2115 I/15, USS Kraken, which was patrolling in the Sunda Strait, received a signal that informed her that the Japanese had capitulated and that offensive operations were to cease immediately.

At 2330 I/15, a signal was received which ordered USS Kraken to leave patrol immediately and proceed to Subic Bay. Course was set to comply with this vessel and USS Kraken cleared the Sunda Strait shortly after 0130 I/16. (32)

21 Aug 1945 Around 0600 I/21, USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN) made rendezvous with USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Capitaine (T/Lt.Cdr. E.S. Friedrick, USN) and their escort USS Rolf (Lt.Cdr. L.E. Hubbell, USNR). They then proceeded to enter Subic Bay.
At Subic Bay, USS Kraken ended her 4th war patrol. At Subic Bay she was taken in hand for refit by USS Anthedon (T/.Capt. R.E. Hawes, USN).

31 Aug 1945 The submarine tender USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Cdr. C.A. Johnson, USN) and the submarines USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Rasher (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Nace, USN), USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta, USN), USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN), USS Becuna (T/Lt.Cdr. W.J. Bush, USN), USS Caiman (T/Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN), USS Blenny (T/Lt.Cdr. C.H. Fuller, Jr., USN), USS Blueback (T/Cdr. M.K. Clementson, USN), USS Boarfish (T/Lt.Cdr. E.C. Blonts, Jr., USN), USS Capitaine (T/Lt.Cdr. E.S. Friedrick, USN), USS Carbonero (T/Cdr. C.L. Murphy, USN), USS Entemedor (T/Cdr. W.R. Smith, Jr., USN), USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) departed Subic Bay. All were to proceed to Pearl Harbour except USS Blenny, USS Blueback and USS Boarfish which were to proceed to Guam.

14 Sep 1945 The submarine tender USS USS Howard W. Gilmore (T/Cdr. C.A. Johnson, USN) and the submarines USS Puffer (T/Lt.Cdr. C.R. Dwyer, USN), USS Rasher (T/Lt.Cdr. C.D. Nace, USN), USS Raton (T/Lt.Cdr. G.F. Gugliotta, USN), USS Baya (T/Lt.Cdr. B.C. Jarvis, USN), USS Becuna (T/Lt.Cdr. W.J. Bush, USN), USS Caiman (T/Cdr. W.L. Fey, Jr., USN), USS Capitaine (T/Lt.Cdr. E.S. Friedrick, USN), USS Carbonero (T/Cdr. C.L. Murphy, USN), USS Entemedor (T/Cdr. W.R. Smith, Jr., USN), USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) arrived at Pearl Harbor from Subic Bay.

15 Sep 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) departed Pearl Harbor for San Francisco.

22 Sep 1945 USS Hardhead (T/Lt.Cdr. J.L. Haines, USN), USS Hawkbill (T/Cdr. W. Scanland, Jr., USN), USS Kraken (T/Cdr. T.H. Henry, USN), USS Lamprey (T/Lt.Cdr. L.B. McDonald, USN), USS Lizardfish (T/Cdr. O.M. Butler, USN) and USS Loggerhead (T/Cdr. R.N. Metcalf, USN) arrived at San Francisco from Pearl Harbor.
